entry.title: Adjustment Costs in the Cosmere
entry.content:
The Cosmere

I hesitate to write a blog post about the Cosmere, given that it is unfinished. Then again, Sanderson’s ambitions are monumental, and most projections estimate that it will take another 25 to 30 years to finish all (or even most) of his planned books. Who knows how life will be by then?

There is a lot to say about his Marvel Cinematic Universe-style project, both good and bad. Overall, I remain deeply impressed by the vision behind the Cosmere, and each new world having both its own unique flavor yet fitting within the broader Cosmere structure. Writing is (so far) uneven and a work in progress; not every character sticks memorably; and magic systems can sometimes become too mechanical or superhero-like for my taste. All in all, though, I am a fan, and will continue reading the books as they appear.

I was prompted to write this blog after finishing Isles of the Emberdark, his latest novel(la). The book has interesting things to say about the upheaval wrought by technology, but it also exemplifies a very Sandersonian trait: to assume away social conflict.

The Emberdark

I won’t try to summarize the entire premise of the Cosmere; go read and (gradually) find out.[^1] Isles of the Emberdark takes place in the spacefaring future of the series, far beyond the timelines of any of the series currently published; but comparable to Tress, the Sunlit Man, and Yumi. Most of the action takes place on (and around) a world called First of the Sun, in the Drominad system. In Arcanum Unbounded, it is speculated that this system was inhabited by an avatar of the Shard Autonomy; actions in the novel (where this avatar is called Patji) corroborate this hypothesis.

First of the Sun is inhabited by island-faring people, who until very recently had a social organization comparable to the Polynesian peoples (which provided the inspiration for the culture). The main inhabited islands – homeisles – are (at the start of the story) on steampower, while the mystical islands of the Pantheon resist technological change. These islands are the domain of trappers – (mostly) men who groom the magical parrots Aviar, who provide symbiotic powers to their human companions. We follow the travails of one trapper, Sixth of the Dusk, who traps the main island of Patji.

Dusk is thrown headfirst into planetary conflict with the arrival of the Ones Above (Scadrian spacefarers). This arrival plunges the world into rapid technological catchup and (some) political change; but curiously, no serious political upheaval. Together with his friend, President Vathi, Dusk races to save his world from intergalactic colonization. In so doing, he rediscovers the mystical past of his people, magic powers granted by Patji only to his people, which eventually make the inhabitants of First of the Sun indispensable and therefore give the potential to stave off enslavement.

Assessment

I liked the book, and I think its transformation from the original novella Sixth of the Dusk is convincing. The world is colorful, the Aviar are nice; I like the exploration of the Emberdark, and I found that this adds more depth and flavor to Shadesmar, which felt a bit bland to me in Stormlight. Remarkably, I think the best part of the book is its main character. Sanderson’s characters tend to suffer from a talkativeness that feel very millennial-American to this European reader; Dusk is refreshingly taciturn.

Its broader place within the Cosmere remains to be seen; yet the hints are intriguing. The book seems to provide evidence for the long-hypothesized transformation of Harmony into Discord, tells us more about dragons and Yolen, and also gives more information about aethers and the Sleepless. All in all, a good book to have.

Technology and Conflict

Sanderson is good at treating magic like technology[^2]; in this novel, he gives his most serious attempt to date of exploring what technological change means for society. Dusk is a trapper on a canoe, outpaced by steamboats and guns; the steamboats and guns are outpaced by the Malwish spaceships and the vast technological change they bring. The analogy to the Patji flora and fauna in the zoo, defanged of their danger and hence of their potency, is on the nose but apt. Many of us, when faced with innovation that threatens to uproot our way of life in the space of years, are left dizzy and uprooted, at risk of drowning in a world that cares nothing for skills that were relevant just a decade ago.

Nowadays, we look to AI or the computer as primary exhibits of this upheaval. But none of this is new; technology has created and destroyed for thousands of years. The speed of upheaval, too, is not new. Phillipp Blom’s magisterial Vertigo Years is a convincing account of how technology completely disoriented European society on the eve of World War I. Where physical strength was the primary basis in producing output for thousands of years, the introduction of electricity made physical labor – and therefore men’s social status – radically uncertain. This sense of displacement manifested itself in various ways; the arms race, the sudden crackdown on homosexuality in the German empire, the renewed concern with birthrates and racial purity. I could go on, but the parallels with present times should make this sufficient, even if the ultimate tragedy of that period does not repeat itself.

Ultimately, this struggle with modernity comes out too clean in Sanderson’s works. I applaud his earnest efforts to focus on mental health and self-development in his novels; many readers will recognize part of their struggle in his depiction of Steris, Shallan, Kaladin, or Dusk. Nevertheless, while group therapy might be a very healthy thing for real-life conditions, it does not make for convincing or satisfactory conflict resolution at societal scale.

Sanderson has a difficult relationship with power structures. In most of his works, there is an absolute monarchy; if there is some form of elected position (like in Emberdark or the Wax & Wayne novels), the plot’s driving conflict always provides an excuse to consolidate power in the hands of the ruler. Elend Venture, angsty aristocrat turned Lord Ruler in the first Mistborn trilogy, idealistically tries democracy before sadly concluding that the time isn’t right. Dalinar Kholin is a tyrant by admission, yet believes that necessary to combat Odium. There is a power fantasy inherent in these setups; simply wishing for absolute power shouldn’t mean that you’ll get it. This is something that the Wheel of Time does beautifully. Rand al’Thor is the most powerful channeler in existence; he commands nations and most of the Aiel. Yet, he never has absolute power; he is always curtailed, countermanded, sidelined. The Aiel, his most loyal following, never unconditionally do what he says; the Maidens mother him, the chiefs and Wise Ones speak back to him even during the worst of his madness.1

This is of course how the world is; even the most personalistic dictatorship has its factions, personal power bases, and feuds. More, perhaps, than a well-functioning democracy. What is missing from Sanderson’s books is this layer between the guys (and girls) at the top and the broader population. Elend’s misadventures with parliament and Vathi’s impending loss of parliamentary support represent about the sum total of meaningful inter- and intra-elite conflict.2 Moreover, we never really get the common man’s perspectives in these books. Wax ponders paternalistically how economic downturn puts pressure on his industrial empire to lay off workers; we never really see the workers’ perspective. This great potential source of social unrest never materializes, because we get inter-planetary conspiracies and a world-destroying bomb instead; a very Marvel-like plot resolution that I fear will detract from the Cosmere’s humanity as its scope expands.

Emberdark does this both better and worse than his earlier works. Better, in the sense that he takes time to think about powerlessness and displacement from the downtrodden’s perspective, rather than from an idealized king’s. Worse, because the entire planet collectively decides to industrialize in the space of five years and this produces no major social conflict whatsoever. Occasional trappers detained by police do not count; the adjustment cost function in Sanderson’s books is not only smooth, it might even be concave rather than convex.3

In the end, Sanderson pulls a rabbit out of his hat by bestowing unique powers on the Drominad people which are non-transferable (their ability to feel the pulse of the Emberdark and thus navigate parts of Shadesmar nobody else can). This deus-ex-machina, which vastly increases their bargaining power, fits with Autonomy’s way of doing things, so is fine to tie off the story; however, on a broader scale, it reduces the novel’s way to speak to the upheaval caused by technology in the real world. Add to that Sanderson’s reluctance to explore social conflict in any shape, and we have a sanitized view of creative destruction, where the creation is recognized broadly through mind-positive communication, and the destruction is reduced to one or two plot points that neatly resolve themselves through the actions of the all-powerful and all-important main characters. This is not wrong per se, but is a missed opportunity; and I hope that Brandon develops his writing for the remaining 30 years to more meaningfully incorporate the darker sides of technology and societal conflict. His writing will be the better for it.

Notes

[^1] Brandon is very enthusiastic about sharing information with his readers, which I mostly find laudable and refreshing. However, his tendency to give spoilers through his ``Words of Brandon’’ is irksome; for instance, I was gripped by the letters exchanged in the epigraphs in The Way of Kings, until I found out online that all mystery about the identities had long been dispelled. Therefore, I advise prospective Cosmere-readers not to read up online until you’ve finished Mistborn I and/or the first arc of Stormlight on your own; let the mystery resolve on its own timing. Then again, it probably cannot be reasonably expected of readers to have to go thirty more years before being “allowed” to find out about Frost and other Yolenish characters.

[^2] Probably too good; I like a bit of mystery, or at least an organic feel to magic. This is what Wurts does very well in The Wars of Light and Shadow. Also Robert Jordan, despite claims by Sanderson that his magic is “hard”, never lets the One Power devolve into mechanical gameplay. At times, reading Stormlight or Mistborn (particularly the later novels) feels like watching an RPG, complete with level-ups and Pokémon-style balance in the different power combinations.

  1. I’ll leave the discussion of Egwene’s parallel rise to power and her obsession with absolute obedience and respect for another time. ↩

  2. Two partial exceptions to this statement are (i) religious factionalism (like in Elantris and Warbreaker) and (ii) military conflict (the Alethi princes jockeying for power). I think neither exception disproves the rule, in particular since his novels always have a way of delivering ultimate power to the executive in such a way that these factions become toothless. Susebron gets his tongue back and hence can wield his enormous arsenal of Breaths to the point of single-handedly stopping entire armies; Dalinar refounds the Knights Radiant to permanently outclass the Alethi skeptics (although I admit that this conflict does drive a good chunk of the plot in the first two-and-a-half novels of Stormlight; perhaps not coincidentally, those are also the best novels he’s written). ↩

  3. Translation: In investment models, a firm contemplates adjusting its capital stock through investment. To generate a meaningful wedge between the firm’s market value and the book value of its capital stock (i.e., Tobin’s Q above 1), the firm must face an adjustment cost function of the form \(\Phi(K)\), which is increasing and convex in its argument (i.e., \(\Phi'(K)>0, \Phi''(K)<0\)). There is a large debate about the role of fixed costs in investment, which would lead to non-convexities in the adjustment cost function; see, for instance, Abel and Eberly (1994) for a unifying treatment. ↩
entry.summary: In which I reflect on Brandon Sanderson's approach to political conflict

entry.title: Moulin Rouge, La Boheme, and the Portrayal of Inequality

link.title: Moulin Rouge, La Boheme, and the Portrayal of Inequality

entry.content:
Intro

Yesterday, I attended the Moulin Rouge musical adaptation, in Utrecht’s Beatrixtheater. I have some thoughts on the execution of the plot, but also think the interest in La Bohème-like stories tells us something interesting about inequality and what dimensions of it are salient.

The Musical

Moulin Rouge is set in fin-de-siècle Paris, where a strapping but poor American “singer-songwriter” named Christian arrives in the city, meets fellow artists/bohemièns, who persuade him to audition one of his songs for star performer Satine in the Moulin Rouge nightclub, in the hopes that will lead to a commission for a full show. Satine, meanwhile, has used her charm and iron determination to move from the gutter to become the “sparkling diamond” of Parisian nightlife. As such, Moulin Rouge’s owner uses her to seduce a wealthy Duke, in order to secure his financial backing for the struggling theater.

The stage is set for the love triangle to emerge: Satine mistakes Christian for the Duke, seduces him, falls in love, only to discover that she has to be the Duke’s mistress. Christian manages the Duke’s backing for the bohemian show, but grows frustrated with being Satine’s secret lover. Satine is torn between love for Christian, sense of duty for saving the theater, and the genuine hope of escaping her destitute background.

The show ends when Satine’s cough develops into full-blown tuberculosis (very La Bohème-like), and Satine dies during the premiere in Christian’s arms. The Duke, so menacing before, vanishes from the plot, and we end with some final dance routines.

Assessment

Visually, I found the musical very striking. The choreography is strong, most of the decors are very effective, and the lighting is well-done. I found the Duke well-performed, as were Satine, the nightclub owner, and the bohemian sidekicks. I was less happy with Christian, who both doesn’t act very well and didn’t sing in tune (particularly the top notes). However, all in all, the production and performer sides of the show were fine.

Not so fine was the music. Instead of writing an original score for the musical (or apparently for the film on which it is based), the music consists of a bizarre medley of pop songs from all over the place. Some of these songs worked, many didn’t. Christian singing a corny rendition of Adele’s Rolling in the Deep after Satine rejected him near the end of Act II was a case in point; all the emotional poignancy is immediately lost, the ambiance of the setting completely forgotten, to the extent that it becomes ridiculous. Also, the variation of songs was not good at all; played at a too-loud volume, the aggressive beats become physically tiresome very quickly. Such a wasted opportunity.

I was struck with parallels to famous operas, like La Bohème (very obviously, given the many “borrowed” plot elements) and La Traviata. There are many operas and musicals with flimsy love triangles masquerading as plot. What saves those works is the quality of the setting, and most of all the music. Here, we saw what happens if the music falls short; the whole product is diminished.1

Love in Times of Cholera

There are interesting aspects to the love story, even if the execution in this rendition wasn’t first-rate. One of Piketty’s most insightful observations in Capital in the Twenty-First Century is to use 19th-century literature as evidence for entrenched wealth inequality; and indeed, the entire plot of Moulin Rouge revolves around the startling gap in resources between the Duke on the one hand and everyone else on the other.

The nature of the class gap, however, deserves some further deliberation. Many critics to Piketty’s thesis have argued that we shouldn’t ultimately care about wealth, but about consumption; for this is what enters agents’ utility functions.2 And you can make a good case that what motivates Satine and her fellow poverty-escapers isn’t so much the Duke’s wealth, but rather his consumption pattern: the fancy dresses, the chateau overlooking the Champs-Elysees, the champagne. Certainly, the musical – a visual medium – drives home the gap in backgrounds by visual aspects of inequality, which almost inevitably reflect consumption inequality rather than wealth inequality per se.

This is in contrast to an operetta like Die lustige Witwe, which revolves (rather incredibly) around the widow’s wealth, since her late husband left her almost the entire stock of the fictional Balkan country Pontevedro’s government debt. Expenditures are not the primary concern here; rather, control over the country’s public finances. But in most cases I can think of, the line between pure wealth inequality and pure consumption inequality is more blurry. Sure, the Duke wears fancier clothes, but this is not the heart of the class gap; even if the bohemians would dress up exactly the same, the difference in bearing and sense of entitlement would surely be different. This reflects of course Bourdieu’s meaning of social and cultural capital, which is intimately tied up with wealth in a way that consumption or income cannot mimick.

Perhaps the most directly salient dimension of inequality explored in the performance is gender inequality. Satine’s looks and wiles are commodified and bartered, by her employer and herself, reducing her to a trophy to be gained. It is interesting to see the extent to which the Duke is driven by his desire to completely own and dominate her; I think this tells us something about how humans visualize power. Even nowadays, when technology can automate increasing numbers of tasks, what to many is the ultimate sign of luxury? PAs, private drivers, underlings – in other words, humans to order around. Hence, perhaps the ultimate reflection of power – from ancient Pharaohs to the current era – is human labor at your service. For quite a few powerful men, as we learn every day in the news, female human labor at your service is perhaps the greatest prize, and the greatest trophy to show others your status.

Another interesting thread running through these works is the sense of despair wrought by disease – tuberculosis, specifically. Satine is ashamed of her condition, it is inconvenient to her performance and distracting when she desperately wants the premiere of Christian’s show to be a success. The secrecy with which she cloaks her illness seems to imply that there is societal judgment about catching such a disease. It is not, apparently, a fate which can strike any believer, like the Black Death was centuries earlier; or at least, if it does strike you you’re not supposed to own up to it. This might be intertwined with the final component of inequality in this story – that of mobility. Poverty, whether measured in a consumption sense or in a wealth sense, shapes people’s very behavior and attitudes towards others, often in self-fulfilling ways. In Terry Pratchett’s Unseen Academicals, there is the metaphor of the crab bucket: a single crab escaping the bucket is dragged down by the crabs still at the bottom, so none can escape. It is a powerful story for how hard it can be to truly escape poverty.3

Satine’s shame for her illness, much like Mimi’s in La Bohème, shouldn’t therefore be seen as shame for the disease in its own right. Rather, she is ashamed of her weakness; ashamed that she hasn’t the means to treat it, nor the time necessary to recover, for so many depend on her and she cannot let them down. This is the crab bucket; her pride won’t allow her to seek help when she very well could have received it, with tragic consequences. This should remind us, quantitatively trained scientists, how much of the most subtle and pernicious harm inflicted by inequality is in how it shapes human perception and interaction. For this reminder, even if the music fell short, we can be grateful to the performers of Moulin Rouge

Notes

  1. In addition, I am less than enthused by the tightly-controlled nature of an international megaproduction like this; each sentence, each gesture, each (nearly-in-tune) note is ruthlessly micromanaged to the point of blandness. This comes across most clearly by the badly-translated lines and the overall American-ness of the corny plot, but you can also feel it from the over-rehearsed nature of the show; spectacular, but it doesn’t breathe. ↩

  2. To me, this is almost tautological, since we axiomatically define agents’ preferences over consumption bundles \(c\) in the first place and then act triumphant when only consumption shows up in the preference ordering \(U(c)\). Moreover, there are actually quite a few circumstances (both in theoryland and empirically), where we let some version of wealth enter agents’ utility functions directly; see Saez and Stantcheva for theory and evidence, and Fella et al. for a recent argument that retirees’ high wealth at death cannot be reconciled with standard bequest motives or precautionary saving and hence must indicate some kind of revealed preference for wealth for its own sake. ↩

  3. It is a testament to Pratchett’s genius that he was able to produce writing this thoughtful even in Unseen Academicals, which otherwise suffers from the tragic impairment of his skills caused by Alzheimer’s disease. Indeed, most of the actual writing in UA was carried out by his assistant Rob Wilkins. ↩
entry.summary: In which I reflect on the dimensions of inequality in art

entry.title: Reflections on Janny Wurts’ *The Wars of Light and Shadow*

link.title: Reflections on Janny Wurts’ *The Wars of Light and Shadow*

entry.content:
Intro

This is the first of (potentially several) blog posts, in which I reflect on things that strike my fancy. One goal in particular will be to connect two passions of mine, economics and fantasy literature. In this first post, I reflect on Janny Wurts’ The Wars of Light and Shadow (WoLaS from now on). I recently finished the final book, Song of the Mysteries, after restarting reading the series somewhere last year.

Spoilers from now on, be warned!

Summary

tWoLaS is an 11-book cycle mainly taking place on the planet of Athera, with main continent Paravia. This is a planet that (we learn throughout the books) has not originally been inhabited by humans; instead, it was first populated by dragons and later also by three special races called Paravians: unicorns, centaurs, and sunchildren. The Paravians are the centerpiece of this world’s history, for they were sent directly by Ath (God, if you will) to bring grace to the world. You see, these dragons had the nasty habit of running amok with their creative magic, which led to countless horrors and destructions, foremost among them several genocidal nightmare races destroying every living thing.

An interesting feature of the setting is that, while it on first glance looks medieval, it is in fact a sci-fi setting. The prime human movers, the seven super-powerful sorcerers known as the Fellowship of the Seven, arrive on Athera over 10,000 years before the books start. Their arrival is caused by the dragons, who magically pull their spaceship on collision course, and subsequently transform these humans into immortal creatures with mind-boggling power. Their task? To use that power to protect Paravian survival at all cost.

And so they do. Over thousands of years, they hunt the drakespawn to extinction. Interestingly, when the dragon imperative doesn’t bind them, they commit themselves to the Law of the Major Balance: a Paravian concept where no “no force of nature should be used without consent, or against the will of another living being”. Throughout the books, we see the sorcerers ask permission of every sentient being before engaging their power. In Wurts’ vision, not only humans or animals have sentience; so do plants and even inanimate matter. All that is, vibrates; consent is aligning the frequency of what you do to be in harmony with a being’s core resonance. Here we see Wurts’ devotion to music, something I will return to.

After safeguarding the Paravians from the drakespawn, the Fellowship’s past comes back to haunt them: the remnants of mankind travel through space following thousands of years of catastrophic interplanetary conflict; conflict instigated in no small part because of the Big Weapon created by seven scientists now known as the Fellowship. Struck with remorse, the Fellowship ask the Paravians if mankind can settle on Athera, taking responsibility for their conduct. What follows is six thousand years of monarchy, where the kings (and the nobility) are those humans who last longest when exposed to the Paravians’ mystic presence. The royal bloodlines are all engineered by the Fellowship to have a dominant trait (justice, compassion, and so on). Ordinary humans live in towns and cities, while the wilds remain proscribed. Technology remains forcibly limited to medieval standards; some humans’ attempts to discover gunpowder get them expelled to a splinter world.

This decision spectacularly backfires, since the experimenters’ offspring eventually create a technology that traps live spirits in mist fields. When the mist fields spiral out of control, this entity – referred to as the Mistwraith – swallows their entire world, devours everyone’s spirit in the process, and oozes back towards Athera to smother that world too. At the same time, a conspiracy of necromancers and the Koriani (the Fellowship’s prime antagonists, more on which later) overthrows the monarchies, resulting in chaos and extermination of the noble clans. In utter chaos, descendants of the royal bloodlines are smuggled to another splinter world. The Paravians depart the mist-cloaked continent for an unknown destination, leaving the understaffed Fellowship to manage affairs.

Centuries later, two of the royal heirs, Lysaer s’Ilessid and Arithon s’Ffalenn, return to Athera and are swept up by the Fellowship. Their elemental power manages to disperse the Mistwraith, restoring sunlight. However, the wraith’s counterstroke curses the two half-brothers to seek to destroy each other whenever they meet. Arithon, being mage-trained, has some control over the curse, but Lysaer succumbs wholesale. Over the next centuries, events spiral out of control, with large-scale war and small-scale tragedy marring Paravia’s surface. Along the way, the dragons and Paravians eventually return to our attention, the Fellowship is restored to its full strength, and at the very end, the Mistwraith is defeated; even if not all its evil has been undone.

Assessment

TWoLaS is an epic fantasy cycle, on the scale of grandeur of a Wheel of Time or Stormlight Archive. The plot spans centuries (with elaborate backstory going back tens of thousands of years), yet remains tightly knit and focused on a core set of characters. The magic is expansive and rich yet never feels contrived or mechanical; I am impressed with how naturally she merges the magical mechanics with her conception of the world and the story. The human tragedy in these stories is very compelling to me. At multiple points in the books I got almost physically unwell because of the agony suffered by Arithon and his friends, or because of Lysaer’s sly duplicity, or the Koriani cruelty.

That said, the books are not easy to digest. This is partly because of its greatest strength: everything is tightly plotted, from start to finish. There is no meandering here; every action from the very first results in a very natural flow of consequences, such that the series reads as the natural unfolding of a very tight beginning. However, this also means that the plot develops slowly, and can even seem repetitive. On Reddit, I found this funny comment which captures this feeling (if a bit harshly). In addition, Wurts’ prose is flowery and rich, often beautiful, but frequently very hard to actually understand. I have lost count of the times where I had to reread a passage multiple times to make sense of what actually was going on, which made the reading experience quite slow and even tiresome at times.

Nevertheless, my overall assessment of the series is very positive. It is deeply impressive to write eleven books that are this tightly integrated, while sacrificing seemingly very little in terms of core vision. The underlying morality moved me deeply; the magic of the setting is beautifully evocative; the worldbuilding is excellent. I recommend this series wholeheartedly.

Ultimately, I think the concepts explored in the series are perhaps the most interesting aspect of the books. With that in mind, let’s get to the analysis.

Analysis

I will focus in my analysis on three themes: technology and progress, politics and human nature, and Wurts’ conception of magic and music.

Technology and Progress

I write this review from the Netherlands, a country where the horrors of polio and cholera are still within living memory yet feel incredibly distant. In the course of the twentieth century, we have managed to improve wellbeing by a staggering amount. Let me be clear: this is a victory for progress through technological change. Yet the flip side of technology is also clear: the same science that brought us improved treatment for cancer also forms the basis for nuclear weapons; our understanding of DNA can both cure disease and be used to foster eugenics. What matters, therefore, is not just technological progress per se but its direction, as forcefully argued by Daron Acemoglu.

Yet, while Acemoglu is much more skeptical of technological change as a force for good than many other growth economists, his message remains ultimately that growth is essential. Moreover, his work on institutions also emphasizes that the ultimate preconditions for prosperity are in our hands; changing institutions is no easy task, but it can be done. Likewise, steering technological change through targeted subsidies can work and has worked; witness the explosion in solar power after decades of subsidies made the technology so cost-effective to outcompete dirty alternatives.

Whether technology’s direction can be successfully steered is a central tension in Wurts’ series too. Unlike Acemoglu, however, the vision espoused by her – through the Fellowship’s ban on any post-medieval technology – clearly views technology as ultimately unsustainable. In tWoLaS, technology is all destruction, no creation. This vision on technology is most visible in the scene in the second book where Fellowship sorcerer Asandir visits the Alestron nobles who have rediscovered gunpowder:

‘I will say plainly that black powder is a first step on the path that led your forebears to destruction.’ Asandir straightened, as if flicked by a creeping small frisson of chill. ‘A first step, and a tiny one, of seeming insignificance. But the insidious progression of change its use will bring is well known to us. The result over time would spoil the green earth, then breed an enslavement of the mind beyond your most dire imagining.’

I find this hard to swallow. Certainly, technology has wreaked enormous destruction; yet it has also created marvels. Sure, Athera’s magnetic lanes mystically ensure good health so that disease and famine are unknown; no tech needed for that. Yet technology is not just about more stuff or even more material wellbeing. It presents the possibility for all of us to explore our talents and calling, not hindered by the strictures of a feudal economy. What would be my possibilities, growing up on Athera? I could work on the fields; I could be a merchant; be a soldier or do some civil service job. There are no centers of learning, surprisingly little space for the arts (more on that below), nor any other of the myriad types of job that one might choose. The Fellowship, supposedly all in favor of human dignity and free choice, condemn ordinary humans like Tarens’ family to poverty and financial fragility by denying society the kind of change that lifts all living standards.

Now, Wurts might find this a failure of the imagination on my behalf; and I will grant that being in the presence of the Paravians and their electromagnetic lanes does a lot for one’s wellbeing. Yet the series is also clear that most humans are not equipped to deal with that at all! Ordinary humans live in proscribed circumstances for thousands of years, out of some academic hope (espoused by Fellowship sorcerer Ciladis) that mankind eventually evolves to be able to live in Paravian grace. When the series closes, that point has not been reached.

While touched on during some points, there is also no serious attempt throughout the series to make this tension into the interesting conflict it could be. The Koriathain’s Prime Matriarch is supposedly motivated by her desire to restore mankind to its technological greatness, a past she can recall since her order’s crystals function like computer storage. This could have set the stage for a really tough moral dilemma: what is the direction we as society should go toward? More technology, with more opportunity yet also the risk of catastrophe? Or a magical Arcadia where opportunity is proscribed yet we are all (supposedly) happy?

In my opinion, if there is one point where the series did not deliver, it is this. This battle over technology’s limits is at the core of all plots hatched by the Koriani to thwart the Fellowship; yet it never resolves satisfactorily. Morriel/Selidie perishes through her misuse of the Waystone (whose self-destruction also conveniently burns the last ships that could return humankind to its former technology levels); Lirenda gets struck moon-eyed by the arrival of a unicorn and promptly repents all her evil plots. This is a missed opportunity, since it feels like Wurts has forced her preference on us as readers, whereas a more ambiguous ending would have both served the plot better and forced us to reflect on the not-so-easy tradeoffs involved here.

Politics and Human Nature

Wurts has frequently extolled the subtlety of her writing.1 Exhibit A: the Fellowship-created monarchy completely shatters our preconceptions of a medieval-style monarchy. Where we might think that these people lord it over those poor townborn suckers, in actual fact they are in service to the people, not the other way around. Their main service: to liaise with the Paravians, a hazardous undertaking; and indeed, an early clue is that Arithon is the 1,504th heir to Rathain in 6,000 years, implying an enormously high deathrate among royalty. Even the ingrained idea that townborn were second-rate citizens since they had no magic is shown to be incorrect, since clan lines used to be more fluid before the Mistwraith. So far, so good: the nobility does hazardous jobs and the ordinary people are not literally discriminated.

I do not think this system of government is as novel as she claims it is; even the most depraved feudal ruler ultimately derived his legitimacy from their defense of the realm. But what is more interesting than the supposed novelty is that the Fellowship created this system de novo. In other words, there is no path dependence here: feudal rule was directly created by the immortal custodians of humankind. Paravians, incidentally, also seem to follow feudal rule (although their exalted state supposedly makes all that more ceremonial than anything).

What I find striking is that this seems to imply that hereditary rule (of an idealized type, to be sure) is an essential precondition for establishing and maintaining paradise. I was actually surprised when I finished Song of the Mysteries when royalty was re-established, after centuries of interregnum. This after we learn about sorcerer Davien’s discontent from the get-go, the bitterness felt by clanborn about their life on the outskirts, the travails of royalty while in service to the land, and so on. Here was an opportunity for something new to emerge from the cataclysms of the Wars between Arithon and Lysaer. And yet again, the perfect solution which we are supposed to embrace unreservedly is to return to the status quo.2

There are several points worth highlighting here. First, one would think that democracy (or at the very least a rapprochement between town and clan) would be superior to the old hierarchy; Tarens’ plot point seemed to work toward that goal. Yet, rather unsubtly, that ideal is quashed because we cannot trust those dastardly townborn with their zealotry and greed; and so the transgressing townborn are all killed by the Paravians (painlessly of course and in full ecstasy from their exposure to the Mysteries, but mass killing even so). Yet another dead end.

Second, this whole contrast between the pure service-led nature of the old monarchy and the corrupt ways of the town councils is in my mind an unsatisfactory way to deal with the intricacies of government. Certainly, corruption is a thing, and special interests can corrupt town councils. It just feels like a copout to magically exempt the clan hierarchies from this fallibility. Clanborn make mistakes (the s’Brydions, Jeynsa), but those are of an individual nature (or perhaps even more insidiously, family traits). There is no serious discussion of opposing interests within clans, misaligned incentives between rulers and their people, or any of the other aspects that make politics a vital plot point of many successful series (Wheel of Time, A Song of Ice and Fire, etc.).

Ultimately, what makes a system of government more or less just is the set of incentives its institutions produce for the diverse powers that be. Here, we have a magically mandated monarchy, where the monarch’s adherence to the Good is supposed to happen because of their bloodline’s magically enabled Virtue; or, if that fails, because their caithdein (shadow behind the throne) will punish their misdeeds; and, if that fails, because Asandir will get angry. As such, it is less a conception of an ideal system of government and more a description of an idealized set of people who should rule us; the form doesn’t seem to matter that much.

As evidence for that claim, consider the Rathain caithdein Cosach in book 10. He does a bad thing (hits Arithon against his will) and is then stripped of his office by Kharadmon of the Seven. In other words, the whole system collapses when the caithdein isn’t up to the job, and the Fellowship needs to browbeat them back into submission. Ultimately, then, this is not a system of judicious checks and balances, but a convenient form for the Fellowship to monitor the power wielders; democracy would be too inconvenient since the Mayors might change too frequently for them to effectively control. Hardly an ideal form of government if you ask me.

The Madisonian quote that “If angels were to govern men, neither external nor internal controls on government would be necessary” seems apt here. And because the rulers in the books are not angels, it feels inadequate to idealize a system of government that rests all power in two people (King and caithdein) and trusts them to be angels, necessitating the Fellowship to come to the rescue when the angels turn out to be human after all.

Magic and Music

The most startling aspect of the series to me was the magic. It is rich, immensely powerful in scope and scale, yet feels entirely natural. Impressively, it also ties in very naturally with her whole conception of reality, that all that is, vibrates. Magic as we call it is simply aligning once’s inner vibration (your core personality) with that of the living thing you wish to engage in your magic. This is why the Law of the Major Balance is important: it ensures harmony (in the musical sense of the word). It also exemplifies a compelling moral point made by the books: each individual is complete and special (‘unique unto Ath’), each individual deserves to be seen and heard. No ‘end justifies the means’ logic here. Intriguingly, even inanimate matter vibrates and therefore deserves to be asked consent.

Wurts is an accomplished bagpiper, and her love of music shows in the books. Most obviously, Arithon’s talent as a musician is ultimately what saves the world. The reimagining of all vibration on the electromagnetic spectrum (and beyond) as a kind of cosmic music is powerful imagery. As such, it is the most seamless integration of music in a fantasy setting I have yet encountered.

However, I am not quite satisfied with her conception of magic/music. The core problem can be traced to her instrument of training. The bagpipe is an individual instrument; although bagpipe ensembles are a thing, the instrument’s timbre is not rich enough to make these ensembles truly different in terms of color. In the books, the instrument of choice is the lyranthe, a kind of lute with silent strings that make it possible to play all kinds of chords and harmonies by yourself. The type of musician we see is your classic medieval troubadour, wandering from town to town, playing some tunes for the ordinary fellows and for the rich hobnobs alike.

There is nothing wrong with this view of music per se, but it limits the possibilities. What is most enriching about music to most of us who practice it is, as conductor Claudio Abbado put it so nicely, “l’amore per fare musicà insieme”. There is no love of making music together in Wurts’ books. This is rather surprising actually, given the whole point about music/magic making us harmonize and empathize with each other. To Wurts, music is an act of individual creativity and autonomy; invariably, Arithon’s, who through his royal gift of Compassion truly understands the nature of the listener. There is no sense that that listener would have to work together with Arithon to create something; nor do we ever see him and his beloved Elaira work together musically/magically to create something new.3

This onesidedness is not limited to humans. The Paravians have divine musicians, notably the sunchildren; and we see them blow their flutes in concert (supposedly in harmony). But there is no value-added to them working together that we learn of; no greater gift of grace or creation enabled through the melding of individual creativity and ingenuity. If ever there would be a template for Wurts’ vision of a harmonious (pun intended) society, it would be that of a chamber choir or orchestra, creating harmony through their individual voices, blending their colors to match each other, producing a combined beauty that is greater than what they could have achieved individually. There is none of that in the books. Arithon plays his guitar lyranthe, he sings, he dances; but it is always him who does the music, not a group, let alone the all-important connection between performer and audience.

Of course, orchestras are not possible in a rural economy that the Fellowship enforces; but even in medieval times, most music was communal, in the form of Gregorian chant and other religious expression. Surely, Ath’s adepts could have served that role in the book. The fact that music remains individualized and onesided (artist to audience, no two-way feedback) limits the strength of Wurts’ otherwise profound writing on music and magic.

Conclusion

Blog posts are supposed to be short and snappy; this is not an auspicious start in that regard. Nevertheless, I feel like Wurts’ vision is deep enough that it deserves to be taken seriously. I remain impressed by the series, my criticisms above notwithstanding. She challenges us to find music in our everyday life, to treat the wellbeing of the smallest among us as essential, and to dare to imagine a better future. I have learned from her books, and I am sure others will too. They deserve to be read.

Notes

  1. As an aside, this was another weakness for me. Sure, some aspects about the series were unexpected, and pleasantly so. But her claims that preconceptions will be shattered seems a bit overblown. I might well be too dense to fully grasp the majesty of her genius; even so, attributes like subtlety are earned, not claimed; it is up to the reader to determine the extent of subtlety. Anything else becomes preachy very quickly. ↩

  2. There is institutional innovation in the sense that Arithon becomes the Paravian-charged King of Paravia, and so becomes (together with Elaira) the first bridge between humans and Paravians (rather Nietzschean when you think about it). I do not find this a major innovation, though, because it just adds a new layer of hierarchy, with even more power invested in the sovereign, who is not only king of the earth but also (because of the dragon’s intervention) immortal and endowed with power on a Fellowship level. This underscores my point in the main text that Wurts seems to wish for idealized philosopher-kings to rule us and doesn’t examine the system of government very closely. We’re supposed to be happy with the outcome because Arithon and Elaira are such good people; but try telling that to a widow of the campaigns waged by the Light. ↩

  3. This claim is a bit too strong: Arithon and Elaira achieve new things through their “gestalt rapport” (a typical Wurtsian flowery phrase), for instance in the healing scene in Ships of Merior or in the Maze of Kewar. My point is rather that this cooperation is purely magical, not musical; moreover, it is a combination of their talents but not clearly something greater than their two talents added up. Similarly, Fellowship sorcerers sometimes work together (Kharadmon and Luhaine, for instance), but from the prose description this feels more like two individual voices that complement each other rather than a blending of two voices into a unique and novel whole. Luhaine is fussy but thorough, Kharadmon is creative yet whimsical, so their magic together has both rigor and creativity. Clearly, this is a constant-returns-to-scale aggregation of their strengths and weaknesses, no “greater than the sum of parts” logic here. ↩
entry.summary: In which I open the secret blog project with reflections on *The Wars of Light and Shadow*
